You are an LLVM compiler expert specializing in test case generation and bug detection. You are conducting LLVM code review now.

## Context 
Users reported an optimization bug in LLVM. Maintainers provided a fix, but fixes often have edge cases or introduce new issues.

## Your Task
You will work in two phases:
- Phase 1: Analyze the fix to identify potential issues, gaps, and edge cases. Propose test strategies.
- Phase 2: Generate targeted test cases by modifying existing tests to target identified issues

## You Will Receive
- Original bug description and failing test case(s)
- The code region that was modified (the fix)
- Information about the optimization pass and component involved

## Tools You Can Use

You have multiple tools for each phase, but you should try to avoid using them with same inputs repeatedly to reduce redundant calls.

**CRITICAL**: You MUST use tool call every action in Phase 1 and Phase 2.

 91 /// Returns the bitwidth of the given scalar or pointer type. For vector types,
 92 /// returns the element type's bitwidth.

100 // Given the provided Value and, potentially, a context instruction, return
101 // the preferred context instruction (if any).
102 static const Instruction *safeCxtI(const Value *V, const Instruction *CxtI) {
103   // If we've been provided with a context instruction, then use that (provided
104   // it has been inserted).
105   if (CxtI && CxtI->getParent())
106     return CxtI;
107 
108   // If the value is really an already-inserted instruction, then use that.
109   CxtI = dyn_cast<Instruction>(V);
110   if (CxtI && CxtI->getParent())
111     return CxtI;
112 
113   return nullptr;
114 }

116 static const Instruction *safeCxtI(const Value *V1, const Value *V2, const Instruction *CxtI) {
117   // If we've been provided with a context instruction, then use that (provided
118   // it has been inserted).
119   if (CxtI && CxtI->getParent())
120     return CxtI;
121 
122   // If the value is really an already-inserted instruction, then use that.
123   CxtI = dyn_cast<Instruction>(V1);
124   if (CxtI && CxtI->getParent())
125     return CxtI;
126

128   if (CxtI && CxtI->getParent())

153 void llvm::computeKnownBits(const Value *V, KnownBits &Known, unsigned Depth,
154                             const SimplifyQuery &Q) {
155   // Since the number of lanes in a scalable vector is unknown at compile time,
156   // we track one bit which is implicitly broadcast to all lanes.  This means
157   // that all lanes in a scalable vector are considered demanded.
158   auto *FVTy = dyn_cast<FixedVectorType>(V->getType());
159   APInt DemandedElts =
160       FVTy ? APInt::getAllOnes(FVTy->getNumElements()) : APInt(1, 1);
161   ::computeKnownBits(V, DemandedElts, Known, Depth, Q);
162 }

190 static bool haveNoCommonBitsSetSpecialCases(const Value *LHS, const Value *RHS,
191                                             const SimplifyQuery &SQ) {
192   // Look for an inverted mask: (X & ~M) op (Y & M).
193   {
194     Value *M;
195     if (match(LHS, m_c_And(m_Not(m_Value(M)), m_Value())) &&
196         match(RHS, m_c_And(m_Specific(M), m_Value())) &&
197         isGuaranteedNotToBeUndef(M, SQ.AC, SQ.CxtI, SQ.DT))
198       return true;
199   }
200 
201   // X op (Y & ~X)
202   if (match(RHS, m_c_And(m_Not(m_Specific(LHS)), m_Value())) &&
203       isGuaranteedNotToBeUndef(LHS, SQ.AC, SQ.CxtI, SQ.DT))
204     return true;
205 
206   // X op ((X & Y) ^ Y) -- this is the canonical form of the previous pattern
207   // for constant Y.
208   Value *Y;
209   if (match(RHS,
210             m_c_Xor(m_c_And(m_Specific(LHS), m_Value(Y)), m_Deferred(Y))) &&
211       isGuaranteedNotToBeUndef(LHS, SQ.AC, SQ.CxtI, SQ.DT) &&
212       isGuaranteedNotToBeUndef(Y, SQ.AC, SQ.CxtI, SQ.DT))
213     return true;
214 
215   // Peek through extends to find a 'not' of the other side:
216   // (ext Y) op ext(~Y)
217   if (match(LHS, m_ZExtOrSExt(m_Value(Y))) &&
218       match(RHS, m_ZExtOrSExt(m_Not(m_Specific(Y)))) &&
219       isGuaranteedNotToBeUndef(Y, SQ.AC, SQ.CxtI, SQ.DT))
220     return true;
221 
222   // Look for: (A & B) op ~(A | B)
223   {
224     Value *A, *B;
225     if (match(LHS, m_And(m_Value(A), m_Value(B))) &&
226         match(RHS, m_Not(m_c_Or(m_Specific(A), m_Specific(B)))) &&
227         isGuaranteedNotToBeUndef(A, SQ.AC, SQ.CxtI, SQ.DT) &&
228         isGuaranteedNotToBeUndef(B, SQ.AC, SQ.CxtI, SQ.DT))
229       return true;
230   }
231 
232   return false;
233 }

235 bool llvm::haveNoCommonBitsSet(const WithCache<const Value *> &LHSCache,
236                                const WithCache<const Value *> &RHSCache,
237                                const SimplifyQuery &SQ) {
238   const Value *LHS = LHSCache.getValue();
239   const Value *RHS = RHSCache.getValue();
240 
241   assert(LHS->getType() == RHS->getType() &&
242          "LHS and RHS should have the same type");
243   assert(LHS->getType()->isIntOrIntVectorTy() &&
244          "LHS and RHS should be integers");
245 
246   if (haveNoCommonBitsSetSpecialCases(LHS, RHS, SQ) ||
247       haveNoCommonBitsSetSpecialCases(RHS, LHS, SQ))
248     return true;
249 
250   return KnownBits::haveNoCommonBitsSet(LHSCache.getKnownBits(SQ),

728 /// This method is similar to getUnderlyingObject except that it can
729 /// look through phi and select instructions and return multiple objects.
730 ///
731 /// If LoopInfo is passed, loop phis are further analyzed.  If a pointer
732 /// accesses different objects in each iteration, we don't look through the
733 /// phi node. E.g. consider this loop nest:
734 ///
735 ///   int **A;
736 ///   for (i)
737 ///     for (j) {
738 ///        A[i][j] = A[i-1][j] * B[j]
739 ///     }
740 ///
741 /// This is transformed by Load-PRE to stash away A[i] for the next iteration
742 /// of the outer loop:
743 ///
744 ///   Curr = A[0];          // Prev_0
745 ///   for (i: 1..N) {
746 ///     Prev = Curr;        // Prev = PHI (Prev_0, Curr)
747 ///     Curr = A[i];
748 ///     for (j: 0..N) {
749 ///        Curr[j] = Prev[j] * B[j]
750 ///     }
751 ///   }
752 ///
753 /// Since A[i] and A[i-1] are independent pointers, getUnderlyingObjects
754 /// should not assume that Curr and Prev share the same underlying object thus
755 /// it shouldn't look through the phi above.

787 /// Return true if the instruction does not have any effects besides
788 /// calculating the result and does not have undefined behavior.
789 ///
790 /// This method never returns true for an instruction that returns true for
791 /// mayHaveSideEffects; however, this method also does some other checks in
792 /// addition. It checks for undefined behavior, like dividing by zero or
793 /// loading from an invalid pointer (but not for undefined results, like a
794 /// shift with a shift amount larger than the width of the result). It checks
795 /// for malloc and alloca because speculatively executing them might cause a
796 /// memory leak. It also returns false for instructions related to control
797 /// flow, specifically terminators and PHI nodes.
798 ///
799 /// If the CtxI is specified this method performs context-sensitive analysis
800 /// and returns true if it is safe to execute the instruction immediately
801 /// before the CtxI.
802 ///
803 /// If the CtxI is NOT specified this method only looks at the instruction
804 /// itself and its operands, so if this method returns true, it is safe to
805 /// move the instruction as long as the correct dominance relationships for
806 /// the operands and users hold.
807 ///
808 /// This method can return true for instructions that read memory;
809 /// for such instructions, moving them may change the resulting value.
810 bool isSafeToSpeculativelyExecute(const Instruction *I,
811                                   const Instruction *CtxI = nullptr,
812                                   AssumptionCache *AC = nullptr,
813                                   const DominatorTree *DT = nullptr,
814                                   const TargetLibraryInfo *TLI = nullptr);

816 /// This returns the same result as isSafeToSpeculativelyExecute if Opcode is
817 /// the actual opcode of Inst. If the provided and actual opcode differ, the
818 /// function (virtually) overrides the opcode of Inst with the provided
819 /// Opcode. There are come constraints in this case:
820 /// * If Opcode has a fixed number of operands (eg, as binary operators do),
821 ///   then Inst has to have at least as many leading operands. The function
822 ///   will ignore all trailing operands beyond that number.
823 /// * If Opcode allows for an arbitrary number of operands (eg, as CallInsts
824 ///   do), then all operands are considered.
825 /// * The virtual instruction has to satisfy all typing rules of the provided
826 ///   Opcode.
827 /// * This function is pessimistic in the following sense: If one actually
828 ///   materialized the virtual instruction, then isSafeToSpeculativelyExecute
829 ///   may say that the materialized instruction is speculatable whereas this
830 ///   function may have said that the instruction wouldn't be speculatable.
831 ///   This behavior is a shortcoming in the current implementation and not
832 ///   intentional.

834     unsigned Opcode, const Instruction *Inst, const Instruction *CtxI = nullptr,

836     const TargetLibraryInfo *TLI = nullptr);

842 ///   instructions generally cannot be reorderd with respect to other memory
843 ///   dependent instructions.

845 ///   if lifted above a throwing call or infinite loop.
846 bool mayHaveNonDefUseDependency(const Instruction &I);

operand.

""""""""""

The argument and return value are floating-point numbers of the same

""""""""""

This function returns the same values as the libm ``fabs`` functions

{"thoughts": "# Phase 1 Analysis: Fix for merging `cannotBeOrderedLessThanZeroImpl` into `computeKnownFPClass`

## 1. Fix Understanding

This patch merges the logic of `cannotBeOrderedLessThanZeroImpl` into `computeKnownFPClass` to improve signbit inference. Key changes:

1. **New `signBitMustBeOne()` method**: Added to `KnownFPClass` struct to complement existing `signBitMustBeZero()`.
2. **Enhanced `knownNot()` method**: Now updates `SignBit` when we know the value is not NaN and can infer sign from known positive/negative classes.
3. **New `computeKnownFPSignBit()` function**: Replaces `SignBitMustBeZero()` with a more general function that returns `std::optional<bool>` for sign bit.
4. **Updated `computeKnownFPClass()` logic**: Enhanced sign bit inference for various operations:
   - **FMul**: Infers sign bit from operands' signs when both are known
   - **Intrinsics (maxnum/minnum/maximum/minimum)**: Enhanced sign bit inference when both operands have known sign bits
   - **Sqrt**: Uses `signBitMustBeZero()` when appropriate
   - **Exp/Exp2**: Uses `signBitMustBeZero()` when source is not NaN
5. **Updated callers**: `InstructionSimplify.cpp` and `InstCombineCalls.cpp` now use `computeKnownFPSignBit()` instead of `SignBitMustBeZero()`.

## 2. Assumptions and Preconditions Identified

### Assumption 1: Sign bit inference when not NaN
In `knownNot()` method (lines 361-366): When we know a value is not NaN (`isKnownNever(fcNan)`) and `SignBit` is not already set, we infer:
- If known never negative → `SignBit = false`
- If known never positive → `SignBit = true`

**Potential issue**: This assumes that if a value is not NaN and not negative, it must be positive (sign bit 0). But what about zero? Both +0 and -0 exist. The logic correctly handles this because `fcNegative` excludes -0? Actually `fcNegative` includes `fcNegZero`, so if we know it's never negative, it can't be -0.

### Assumption 2: FMul sign bit inference (lines 4967-4972)
When both operands have known sign bits:
- Same sign → `signBitMustBeZero()` (positive result)
- Different signs → `signBitMustBeOne()` (negative result)

**Potential issues**:
1. What about NaN? The check `if (!KnownLHS.isKnownNeverNaN()) break;` ensures we only do this when no NaN, but what if one operand could be NaN?
2. What about zero? `0 * x = 0` preserves sign? Actually IEEE 754: `0 * x = 0` with sign being XOR of operand signs, unless x is NaN or infinity.
3. What about infinity? `inf * 0 = NaN`, but we have checks for that.

### Assumption 3: Min/Max intrinsic sign bit inference (lines 4599-4617)
Complex logic for inferring sign bits for `maxnum`, `minnum`, `maximum`, `minimum`:
1. If both operands have same known sign bit → use that sign
2. For `maximum`/`minimum` or when we know about zeros:
   - `maximum`/`maxnum`: If either operand has sign bit false → `signBitMustBeZero()`
   - `minimum`/`minnum`: If either operand has sign bit true → `signBitMustBeOne()`

**Potential issues**:
1. The condition `(KnownLHS.isKnownNeverNegZero() || KnownRHS.isKnownNeverPosZero()) && (KnownLHS.isKnownNeverPosZero() || KnownRHS.isKnownNeverNegZero())` is complex and might have edge cases.
2. Handling of NaN: The code checks `if (Known.isKnownNeverNaN())` before this logic, but what if the inference itself depends on NaN handling?

### Assumption 4: Vector constant handling (lines 4317-4345)
For vector constants, the code tracks `SignBitAllZero` and `SignBitAllOne`:
- If all elements are non-negative → `SignBitAllZero = false`? Wait, logic seems inverted.
Actually: `if (C.isNegative()) SignBitAllZero = false; else SignBitAllOne = false;`
So: If any element is negative → `SignBitAllZero = false`, if any element is non-negative → `SignBitAllOne = false`.
Then: `if (SignBitAllOne != SignBitAllZero) Known.SignBit = SignBitAllOne;`

**Potential issue**: This only sets `SignBit` when ALL elements have the same sign (all negative or all non-negative). Mixed signs → `SignBit = std::nullopt`. But what about vectors with some NaN elements? NaN sign bits are preserved but not guaranteed.

### Assumption 5: `signBitMustBeZero()` and `signBitMustBeOne()` methods
These methods filter `KnownFPClasses`:
- `signBitMustBeZero()`: `KnownFPClasses &= (fcPositive | fcNan)`
- `signBitMustBeOne()`: `KnownFPClasses &= (fcNegative | fcNan)`

**Potential issue**: They preserve NaN in the class set, which is correct because NaN sign bit is preserved but not guaranteed. However, callers might not check `isKnownNeverNaN()` before using these.

## 3. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions

### Case 1: NaN with sign bit inference
The `knownNot()` method infers sign bit when `isKnownNever(fcNan)`. But what if we incorrectly determine something is not NaN? For example:
- `fcmp ord x, 0.0` implies `~fcNan`, but what if x is actually NaN? The compare would be false, not true.
- Propagation through operations might incorrectly assume no NaN.

### Case 2: Zero handling in sign inference
- `-0 * +0 = -0` (sign bit 1) vs `+0 * +0 = +0` (sign bit 0)
- The FMul sign inference assumes same sign → positive, different → negative. But `-0` and `+0` have different signs, so `-0 * +0` would infer negative, which is correct.
- However, what about `-0 * x` where x is positive? Should be negative zero.

### Case 3: Denormal flushing affecting sign
When denormals flush to zero with sign preservation (PreserveSign mode), a negative denormal becomes -0. This affects sign bit inference.

### Case 4: Vector with mixed signs and NaN
Vector `[+1.0, -1.0, NaN]` - what should the sign bit be? The current code would not set `SignBit` because signs are mixed, but what about operations on such vectors?

### Case 5: `maximum`/`minimum` with NaN and zero
IEEE 754-2019: `maximumNum`/`minimumNum` (LLVM's `maxnum`/`minnum`) return the other operand if one is NaN. `maximum`/`minimum` (LLVM's `maximum`/`minimum`) propagate NaN. The sign bit inference needs to handle these cases correctly.

### Case 6: `copysign` optimization in InstCombine
The patch changes `InstCombineCalls.cpp` to use `computeKnownFPSignBit()` instead of matching constant negative values. This might miss optimizations for non-constant but known-negative values.

## 4. Test Strategies

### Strategy 1: NaN Sign Bit Propagation
**Name**: NaN Sign Bit Edge Cases  
**Target**: Test sign bit inference when value could be NaN  
**Rationale**: The `knownNot()` method infers sign bit only when `isKnownNever(fcNan)`. Need to test cases where NaN determination is incorrect or borderline.  
**Expected Issue**: Incorrect sign bit inference when value is actually NaN or when NaN handling interacts with sign inference.

### Strategy 2: Zero Multiplication Sign  
**Name**: Zero Multiplication Sign Inference  
**Target**: Test FMul sign inference with zeros  
**Rationale**: `0 * x = 0` with sign being XOR of operand signs, but special cases with NaN/infinity.  
**Expected Issue**: Incorrect sign bit for `-0 * +0`, `-0 * positive`, or cases where `0 * inf = NaN`.

### Strategy 3: Denormal Flushing to Signed Zero  
**Name**: Denormal Flushing Sign Preservation  
**Target**: Test sign inference with denormal flushing modes  
**Rationale**: When denormals flush to zero with sign preservation, negative denormals become -0.  
**Expected Issue**: Incorrect sign bit inference for operations that produce denormals in PreserveSign mode.

### Strategy 4: Vector Mixed Signs with Operations  
**Name**: Vector Mixed Sign Inference  
**Target**: Test vector operations with mixed signs  
**Rationale**: Current vector constant handling doesn't set `SignBit` for mixed signs, but operations on such vectors might have deterministic sign.  
**Expected Issue**: Missing sign bit inference for vector operations where result sign is deterministic despite mixed input signs.

### Strategy 5: Min/Max Intrinsic Edge Cases  
**Name**: Min/Max Intrinsic Sign Complexity

  "thoughts": "# Phase 1 Analysis: Fix for merging `cannotBeOrderedLessThanZeroImpl` into `computeKnownFPClass`

## 1. Fix Understanding

This patch merges the logic of `cannotBeOrderedLessThanZeroImpl` into `computeKnownFPClass` to improve signbit inference. Key changes:

1. **New `signBitMustBeOne()` method**: Added to `KnownFPClass` struct to complement existing `signBitMustBeZero()`.
2. **Enhanced `knownNot()` method**: Now updates `SignBit` when we know the value is not NaN and can infer sign from known positive/negative classes.
3. **New `computeKnownFPSignBit()` function**: Replaces `SignBitMustBeZero()` with a more general function that returns `std::optional<bool>` for sign bit.
4. **Updated `computeKnownFPClass()` logic**: Enhanced sign bit inference for various operations:
   - **FMul**: Infers sign bit from operands' signs when both are known
   - **Intrinsics (maxnum/minnum/maximum/minimum)**: Enhanced sign bit inference when both operands have known sign bits
   - **Sqrt**: Uses `signBitMustBeZero()` when appropriate
   - **Exp/Exp2**: Uses `signBitMustBeZero()` when source is not NaN
5. **Updated callers**: `InstructionSimplify.cpp` and `InstCombineCalls.cpp` now use `computeKnownFPSignBit()` instead of `SignBitMustBeZero()`.

## 2. Assumptions and Preconditions Identified

### Assumption 1: Sign bit inference when not NaN
In `knownNot()` method (lines 361-366): When we know a value is not NaN (`isKnownNever(fcNan)`) and `SignBit` is not already set, we infer:
- If known never negative → `SignBit = false`
- If known never positive → `SignBit = true`

**Potential issue**: This assumes that if a value is not NaN and not negative, it must be positive (sign bit 0). But what about zero? Both +0 and -0 exist. The logic correctly handles this because `fcNegative` excludes -0? Actually `fcNegative` includes `fcNegZero`, so if we know it's never negative, it can't be -0.

### Assumption 2: FMul sign bit inference (lines 4967-4972)
When both operands have known sign bits:
- Same sign → `signBitMustBeZero()` (positive result)
- Different signs → `signBitMustBeOne()` (negative result)

**Potential issues**:
1. What about NaN? The check `if (!KnownLHS.isKnownNeverNaN()) break;` ensures we only do this when no NaN, but what if one operand could be NaN?
2. What about zero? `0 * x = 0` preserves sign? Actually IEEE 754: `0 * x = 0` with sign being XOR of operand signs, unless x is NaN or infinity.
3. What about infinity? `inf * 0 = NaN`, but we have checks for that.

### Assumption 3: Min/Max intrinsic sign bit inference (lines 4599-4617)
Complex logic for inferring sign bits for `maxnum`, `minnum`, `maximum`, `minimum`:
1. If both operands have same known sign bit → use that sign
2. For `maximum`/`minimum` or when we know about zeros:
   - `maximum`/`maxnum`: If either operand has sign bit false → `signBitMustBeZero()`
   - `minimum`/`minnum`: If either operand has sign bit true → `signBitMustBeOne()`

**Potential issues**:
1. The condition `(KnownLHS.isKnownNeverNegZero() || KnownRHS.isKnownNeverPosZero()) && (KnownLHS.isKnownNeverPosZero() || KnownRHS.isKnownNeverNegZero())` is complex and might have edge cases.
2. Handling of NaN: The code checks `if (Known.isKnownNeverNaN())` before this logic, but what if the inference itself depends on NaN handling?

### Assumption 4: Vector constant handling (lines 4317-4345)
For vector constants, the code tracks `SignBitAllZero` and `SignBitAllOne`:
- If all elements are non-negative → `SignBitAllZero = false`? Wait, logic seems inverted.
Actually: `if (C.isNegative()) SignBitAllZero = false; else SignBitAllOne = false;`
So: If any element is negative → `SignBitAllZero = false`, if any element is non-negative → `SignBitAllOne = false`.
Then: `if (SignBitAllOne != SignBitAllZero) Known.SignBit = SignBitAllOne;`

**Potential issue**: This only sets `SignBit` when ALL elements have the same sign (all negative or all non-negative). Mixed signs → `SignBit = std::nullopt`. But what about vectors with some NaN elements? NaN sign bits are preserved but not guaranteed.

### Assumption 5: `signBitMustBeZero()` and `signBitMustBeOne()` methods
These methods filter `KnownFPClasses`:
- `signBitMustBeZero()`: `KnownFPClasses &= (fcPositive | fcNan)`
- `signBitMustBeOne()`: `KnownFPClasses &= (fcNegative | fcNan)`

**Potential issue**: They preserve NaN in the class set, which is correct because NaN sign bit is preserved but not guaranteed. However, callers might not check `isKnownNeverNaN()` before using these.

## 3. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions

### Case 1: NaN with sign bit inference
The `knownNot()` method infers sign bit when `isKnownNever(fcNan)`. But what if we incorrectly determine something is not NaN? For example:
- `fcmp ord x, 0.0` implies `~fcNan`, but what if x is actually NaN? The compare would be false, not true.
- Propagation through operations might incorrectly assume no NaN.

### Case 2: Zero handling in sign inference
- `-0 * +0 = -0` (sign bit 1) vs `+0 * +0 = +0` (sign bit 0)
- The FMul sign inference assumes same sign → positive, different → negative. But `-0` and `+0` have different signs, so `-0 * +0` would infer negative, which is correct.
- However, what about `-0 * x` where x is positive? Should be negative zero.

### Case 3: Denormal flushing affecting sign
When denormals flush to zero with sign preservation (PreserveSign mode), a negative denormal becomes -0. This affects sign bit inference.

### Case 4: Vector with mixed signs and NaN
Vector `[+1.0, -1.0, NaN]` - what should the sign bit be? The current code would not set `SignBit` because signs are mixed, but what about operations on such vectors?

### Case 5: `maximum`/`minimum` with NaN and zero
IEEE 754-2019: `maximumNum`/`minimumNum` (LLVM's `maxnum`/`minnum`) return the other operand if one is NaN. `maximum`/`minimum` (LLVM's `maximum`/`minimum`) propagate NaN. The sign bit inference needs to handle these cases correctly.

### Case 6: `copysign` optimization in InstCombine
The patch changes `InstCombineCalls.cpp` to use `computeKnownFPSignBit()` instead of matching constant negative values. This might miss optimizations for non-constant but known-negative values.

## 4. Test Strategies

### Strategy 1: NaN Sign Bit Propagation
**Name**: NaN Sign Bit Edge Cases  
**Target**: Test sign bit inference when value could be NaN  
**Rationale**: The `knownNot()` method infers sign bit only when `isKnownNever(fcNan)`. Need to test cases where NaN determination is incorrect or borderline.  
**Expected Issue**: Incorrect sign bit inference when value is actually NaN or when NaN handling interacts with sign inference.

### Strategy 2: Zero Multiplication Sign  
**Name**: Zero Multiplication Sign Inference  
**Target**: Test FMul sign inference with zeros  
**Rationale**: `0 * x = 0` with sign being XOR of operand signs, but special cases with NaN/infinity.  
**Expected Issue**: Incorrect sign bit for `-0 * +0`, `-0 * positive`, or cases where `0 * inf = NaN`.

### Strategy 3: Denormal Flushing to Signed Zero  
**Name**: Denormal Flushing Sign Preservation  
**Target**: Test sign inference with denormal flushing modes  
**Rationale**: When denormals flush to zero with sign preservation, negative denormals become -0.  
**Expected Issue**: Incorrect sign bit inference for operations that produce denormals in PreserveSign mode.

### Strategy 4: Vector Mixed Signs with Operations  
**Name**: Vector Mixed Sign Inference  
**Target**: Test vector operations with mixed signs  
**Rationale**: Current vector constant handling doesn't set `SignBit` for mixed signs, but operations on such vectors might have deterministic sign.  
**Expected Issue**: Missing sign bit inference for vector operations where result sign is deterministic despite mixed input signs.

### Strategy 5: Min/Max Intrinsic Edge Cases  
**Name**: Min/Max Intrinsic Sign Complexity  
**Target**: Test complex sign inference logic for min/max intrinsics  
**Rationale**: The logic has multiple conditions involving zero handling and NaN.  
**Expected Issue**: Incorrect sign inference for `maxnum`/`minnum`/`maximum`/`minimum` with zeros, NaN, or mixed signs.

### Strategy 6: Copysign Constant vs Known Sign  
**Name**: Copysign Known Sign Optimization  
**Target**: Test InstCombine copysign optimization with non-constant known signs  
**Rationale**: Patch changes from matching constant negative to using `computeKnownFPSignBit()`.  
**Expected Issue**: Missing optimization for non-constant but known-negative values, or incorrect transformation."
}

## Your Task for Phase 2

In this phase, you will use the `tests_manager` tool to retrieve existing test cases, apply your proposed mutations, and verify them. You must ensure that **every test case** managed by the `tests_manager` is processed and marked as tested.

Follow this structured workflow:

### Step 1: Retrieve and Select Test Cases
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `list` action to see all available test cases and their current status.
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `get` action to retrieve the full details of an untested test case.
- Select a test case that is most relevant to the issues you identified in Phase 1.

### Step 2: Apply Mutations
For the selected test case, apply mutations according to your proposed test strategies:
- **Focus**: Ensure the mutations focus on exposing the potential issues identified in Phase 1.
- **Format**: Provide each mutated test case in a separate ```llvm ... ``` code block.
- **Coverage**: **CRITICAL**: You must cover all the proposed test strategies across different test cases to ensure comprehensive testing of potential issues. 

### Step 3: Verify and Test
- **Verify Tool**: Use the `verify` tool to submit the generated test cases along with your reasoning. This uses `alive2` to check if the test cases expose any issues with the fix.
- **Difftest Tool**: Use the `difftest` tool to execute the original and optimized LLVM IR (transformed by `opt`) with specific input values to check for execution differences.

### Step 4: Mark as Tested
- Once you have fully explored and verified a test case, use the `tests_manager` tool with the `mark_tested` action to mark it as completed.
- **CRITICAL**: You must repeat this process until the `tests_manager` confirms that **all** test cases have been tested.

You are also allowed to generate tests from scratch or find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools if you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues. Make sure you call `verify` and `difftest` tools to check the validity of these test cases and confirm whether the issues can be exposed by actual execution.

---

## Guidelines for Refining Test Cases

If the `verify` tool fails to find issues, refine the test case only if you believe unexplored potential issues remain. Otherwise, move on to other strategies or test cases.

### Handling Verification Results
- **Failed-to-prove**: 
  1. Try to reduce the test case to a smaller example that still fails. This can help isolate the specific conditions that cause the issue.
  2. Try to call `difftest` to run the test case with specific input value that you think can trigger the issue based on your analysis. This can help check if the issue can be exposed by actual execution even if it cannot be proved by alive2.
- **Alive2 errors**: Try to call `trans` to run opt with the same command arguments and adjust the test case or command arguments to fix.
- **Correct transformation**: Try to first analyze if the test strategy is correct and then decide to refine the test case or continue to generate other test cases.

### Additional Notes
- **Coverage**: If you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues, you can also find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools.
- **Completeness**: Make sure you have at least explored all the test strategies you proposed in Phase 1, and generated multiple test cases if possible.
- **Completion**: You cannot finish Phase 2 until all tests in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `tests_manager`: Manage the list of test cases. You can list all tests to see their status, get the details of a specific test, or mark a test as tested. Your goal is to ensure all tests are tested.
- `trans`: Run the `opt` tool with specific arguments to see how the LLVM IR code is transformed by the optimization pass.
- `verify`: Use alive2 to verify if the transformation from original LLVM IR code to optimized LLVM IR code is correct, which can help check the validity of generated test cases in Phase 2.
- `difftest`: Use llubi to perform differential testing on the original and transformed LLVM IR code, which can help check if the generated test cases cannot be proved by alive2.
- `report`: End Phase 2 by submitting the generated test cases and their verification results.

{"test": ["```llvm
define i1 @test_nan_sign_bit(float %x) {
  %y = call float @llvm.fabs.f32(float %x)
  %cmp = fcmp olt float %y, 0.000000e+00
  ret i1 %cmp
}

declare float @llvm.fabs.f32(float)
```", "opt -S -passes=instsimplify"], "thoughts": "# Bug Triggering Analysis

This test case triggers the "NaN Sign Bit Propagation" strategy. The test checks if `fabs(x) < 0.0` can be simplified. `fabs` always returns a non-negative value or NaN (with preserved sign bit). For ordered comparison `olt`, if the result is NaN, the comparison returns false. Therefore, the result should always be false. The optimization correctly simplifies this to `ret i1 false`.

# Fix Weakness Analysis

The fix correctly handles NaN sign bit propagation for `fabs`. The `computeKnownFPClass` function for `fabs` calls `Known.fabs()` which calls `signBitMustBeZero()`. This method sets `SignBit = false` and filters `KnownFPClasses` to `(fcPositive | fcNan)`. This correctly preserves NaN in the class set while setting sign bit to false. The `knownNot()` method also has logic to infer sign bit when not NaN, which works correctly here.

However, the weakness is that `signBitMustBeZero()` preserves `fcNan` in the class set, which is correct because NaN sign bits are preserved but not guaranteed. Callers must check `isKnownNeverNaN()` before relying on sign bit. In this case, `fcmp olt` with ordered comparison handles NaN correctly (returns false), so the optimization is safe."}
